article:
Chunk # 0.
Mitochondrial Structure and Function Are Disrupted by
Standard Isolation Methods
Martin Picard1, Tanja Taivassalo1, Darmyn Ritchie2, Kathryn J Wright2, Melissa M Thomas2, Caroline
Romestaing3, Russell T Hepple2*
1Department of Kinesiology, McGill University, Montreal, Quebec, Canada, 2Muscle and Aging Laboratory, Faculty of Kinesiology and Faculty of Medicine, University of
Calgary, Calgary, Alberta, Canada, 3Laboratoire de Physiologie Inte ´grative, Cellulaire et Mole ´culaire, Universite ´de Lyon, Villeurbanne, France
Abstract
Mitochondria regulate critical components of cellular function via ATP production, reactive oxygen species production, Ca2+
handling and apoptotic signaling Two classical methods exist to study mitochondrial function of skeletal muscles: isolated
mitochondria and permeabilized myofibers Whereas mitochondrial isolation removes a portion of the mitochondria fromtheir cellular environment, myofiber permeabilization preserves mitochondrial morphology and functional interactions with
other intracellular components Despite this, isolated mitochondria remain the most commonly used method to infer in vivo
mitochondrial function In this study, we directly compared measures of several key aspects of mitochondrial function inboth isolated mitochondria and permeabilized myofibers of rat gastrocnemius muscle Here we show that mitochondrialisolation i) induced fragmented organelle morphology; ii) dramatically sensitized the permeability transition pore sensitivity
to a Ca
2+challenge; iii) differentially altered mitochondrial respiration depending upon the respiratory conditions; and iv)
dramatically increased H 2O2production These alterations are qualitatively similar to the changes in mitochondrial structure
and function observed in vivo after cellular stress-induced mitochondrial fragmentation, but are generally of much greater
magnitude Furthermore, mitochondrial isolation markedly altered electron transport chain protein stoichiometry Collectively, our results demonstrate that isolated mitochondria possess functional characteristics that differ fundamentallyfrom those of intact mitochondria in permeabilized myofibers Our work and that of others underscores the importance ofstudying mitochondrial function in tissue preparations where mitochondrial structure is preserved and all mitochondria are
represented Citation: Picard M, Taivassalo T, Ritchie D, Wright KJ, Thomas MM, et al (2011) Mitochondrial Structure and Function Are Disrupted by Standard Isolation
Methods PLoS ONE 6(3): e18317 doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0018317
Editor: Michael Polymenis, Texas A&M University, United States of America
Received January 12, 2011; Accepted February 24, 2011; Published March 28, 2011

Scholar Award from the Alberta Heritage Foundation for Medical Research, MP holds a PhD fellowship from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Researc h
Council of Canada and is a CIHR Fellow in Systems Biology TT is a Chercheur Boursier Junior I Research Scholar from the Fonds de Recherche en Sante ´du Que ´bec The funders had no role in study design, data collection and analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript Competing Interests: The authors have declared that no competing interests exist * E-mail: hepple@ucalgary.ca
Introduction
Mitochondria are key regulators of cellular function and hence
their dysfunction is implicated in the pathogenesis of manydiseases [1,2,3,4] and the very process of aging itself [5,6] For thisreason, the study of mitochondrial function has become central toa wide variety of clinical and basic science research A powerfultool to investigate mitochondrial function was developed morethan fifty years ago by Chance and Williams (1956), involving theisolation of mitochondria from skeletal muscle This method allowsthe recovery of a relatively pure mitochondrial fraction, throughfirst homogenizing a fresh muscle sample and then purifying themitochondria through a series of differential centrifugation steps[7] Notably, this in vitro approach allowed elucidation of the
nature of the tricarboxilic cycle (Krebs cycle) in the 1960’s [8] andit continues to this day to be used widely to study a variety ofaspects of mitochondrial biology in skeletal muscle, includingmitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) function[9,10], respiratory capacity [9,10,11], reactive oxygen species(ROS) production [10,12,13], mitochondrial protein import andassembly [14,15] and the mitochondrial genome and proteome[16] Despite the widespread adoption of this technique, standard
isolation methods retrieve a low (generally 20–40% of total)
fraction of the total mitochondrial content from muscle[17,18,19,20] For this reason, isolated mitochondria studiesnecessitate relatively large amounts of fresh tissue and have beensuggested to lead to potential bias because of selective represen-tation of the entire mitochondrial pool [21] Another experimental method to study mitochondrial function
in muscle was subsequently developed by Saks and colleagues(1998), which involved the preparation of permeabilized myofi-bers, or skinned fibers This method entails manual separation ofmuscle myofibers, followed by selective permeabilization of thesarcolemma, leaving $95% of all mitochondria intact within the
normal cytoarchitechtural environment [22,23,24] Although thismethod is gaining in popularity, there remains limited data
comparing this approach to isolated mitochondria, with studies
thus far having focused only on respiration [18,21,23]

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "Two classical methods exist to study mitochondrial function of skeletal muscles: isolated mitochondria and permeabilized myofibers."
2. "Mitochondrial isolation removes a portion of the mitochondria from their cellular environment."
3. "Myofiber permeabilization preserves mitochondrial morphology and functional interactions with other intracellular components."
4. "Isolated mitochondria remain the most commonly used method to infer in vivo mitochondrial function."
5. "In this study, we directly compared measures of several key aspects of mitochondrial function in both isolated mitochondria and permeabilized myofibers of rat gastrocnemius muscle."
6. "Mitochondrial isolation induced fragmented organelle morphology."
7. "Mitochondrial isolation dramatically sensitized the permeability transition pore sensitivity to a Ca2+ challenge."
8. "Mitochondrial isolation differentially altered mitochondrial respiration depending upon the respiratory conditions."
9. "Mitochondrial isolation dramatically increased H2O2 production."
10. "These alterations are qualitatively similar to the changes in mitochondrial structure and function observed in vivo after cellular stress-induced mitochondrial fragmentation, but are generally of much greater magnitude."
11. "Furthermore, mitochondrial isolation markedly altered electron transport chain protein stoichiometry."
12. "Our results demonstrate that isolated mitochondria possess functional characteristics that differ fundamentally from those of intact mitochondria in permeabilized myofibers."
13. "Our work and that of others underscores the importance of studying mitochondrial function in tissue preparations where mitochondrial structure is preserved and all mitochondria are represented."

**Conclusion:** The article discusses the comparison between isolated mitochondria and permeabilized myofibers for studying mitochondrial function. It highlights the disadvantages of mitochondrial isolation, including induced fragmented organelle morphology, increased sensitivity to Ca2+ challenge, differential alteration of mitochondrial respiration, increased H2O2 production, and altered electron transport chain protein stoichiometry. The article emphasizes the importance of studying mitochondrial function in tissue preparations where mitochondrial structure is preserved and all mitochondria are represented. However, the article does not provide a detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation.

summary: Revised Summary: Researchers from McGill University's Department of Kinesiology in Montreal, the Muscle and Aging Laboratory at the University of Calgary, and the Laboratoire de Physiologie Intégrative at the Université de Lyon in Villeurbanne conducted a study on the effects of standard isolation methods on the structure and function of mitochondria in skeletal muscles. The study compared two methods of studying mitochondrial function: isolated mitochondria and permeabilized myofibers. The researchers found that mitochondrial isolation induced fragmented organelle morphology, increased sensitivity to the permeability transition pore, altered mitochondrial respiration depending on the conditions, and dramatically increased hydrogen peroxide production. These changes are similar to those observed in vivo after cellular stress-induced mitochondrial fragmentation, but are more pronounced. Additionally, mitochondrial isolation markedly altered electron transport chain protein stoichiometry. The study emphasizes the importance of studying mitochondrial function in tissue preparations, such as muscle myofibers, to preserve mitochondrial structure and accurately represent all mitochondria. The research has implications for understanding respiratory conditions and electron transport chain protein stoichiometry in skeletal muscles.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "We first examined by confocal microscopy the morphology of isolated mitochondria from the mixed region of gastrocnemius muscle using Mitotracker Red. Contrary to well-documented reticular mitochondrial morphology in skeletal muscles, isolated organelles showed a consistent spherical appearance and a relatively homogenous size distribution."
2. "We also measured mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake, with strikingly different dynamics observed between preparations. The Ca2+ uptake data indicate two major points. First, upon Ca2+ stress, most or all isolated mitochondria undergo mPTP opening almost simultaneously (within 5-10 seconds), whereas in permeabilized myofibers, mitochondria exhibiting a broad range of sensitivities undergo mPTP opening at different times (several minutes apart), causing a gradual and progressive inversion of the Ca2+ uptake signal. Second, we also found that time to mPTP opening was 98% shorter in isolated mitochondria compared to permeabilized myofibers (21 vs 977 seconds, respectively) and the amount of Ca2+ necessary to trigger opening of the mPTP was 42% lower in the isolated preparations, demonstrating a marked sensitization of the mPTP to a Ca2+ challenge in isolated mitochondria."
3. "To address the effect of experimental preparation on mitochondrial respiration, mitochondrial O2 consumption was measured during a sequential substrate addition protocol. Compared to permeabilized myofibers, respiration of isolated mitochondria was lower under basal (77% lower) and state II (GM; 53% lower) conditions. However, comparison of state 3 respiration, after activation of both complex I (by glutamate and malate) and complex II (by succinate), revealed similar maximal respiration rates between the two methods. Conversely, direct stimulation of Complex IV yielded an 82% higher respiration rate in isolated mitochondria."
4. "Respiratory control ratio (RCR), defined as the ratio of state 3 (with ADP) to state 2 respiration (without ADP), was also 1.1-fold higher in isolated mitochondria than in permeabilized myofibers."
5. "To determine the relative activity of each complex, we calculated the stoichiometry of respiration rates between complexes I, II, and IV. We found a reduced activity of complex I relative to that of complex II and complex IV in isolated mitochondria compared to permeabilized myofibers."
6. "To investigate the relative abundance of electron transport chain complexes between preparations, we performed Western blot experiments."

**Conclusion:**
The article provides several steps for mitochondrial isolation and characterization. The steps include examining the morphology of isolated mitochondria, measuring mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake, measuring mitochondrial respiration under different conditions, calculating the stoichiometry of respiration rates between complexes, and performing Western blot experiments. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation, and there are some gaps in the description of the steps.

summary: Standard mitochondrial isolation procedures raise concerns about selective and low yield of mitochondria, disruption of the mitochondrial network structure, and lack of interaction with other cellular compartments. In this study, we compared isolated mitochondria with permeabilized myofibers to assess their impact on mitochondrial function. We measured sensitivity to mPTP opening, respiration, and H2O2 generation as indicators of mitochondrial function. We also examined mitochondrial structure and electron transport chain stoichiometry. Our findings reveal significant differences in mitochondrial morphology, mPTP opening dynamics, and Ca2+ sensitivity between isolated mitochondria and permeabilized myofibers. Additionally, isolated mitochondria showed lower respiration rates under basal and state II conditions but similar maximal respiration rates compared to permeabilized myofibers. The stoichiometry of respiration rates between complexes I, II, and IV was also altered in isolated mitochondria. These results provide insights into the impact of isolation procedures on mitochondrial function and structure.

Quantification of blots indicated markeddifferences in the relative abundance of complex I, II, III and
IV between preparations (Figure 4A) Further analysis of the
Western blots revealed intermediate protein size bands in isolatedmitochondrial preparations, contrasting with the discrete bands
corresponding to the predicted molecular weights of the subunits
detected by the antibody cocktail in whole muscle (Figure 4B) Mitochondrial H 2O2release
To address the effect of mitochondrial isolation on mitochon-
drial H 2O2production under different energetic states, we again
used a sequential substrate addition protocol When expressed in
relation to a marker of mitochondrial content (complex IVactivity), H
2O2production was 5–10 fold higher in isolated
mitochondria than in permeabilized myofibers (Figure 5A) and
when expressed per respiration rate and therefore normalized perelectron flow within the electron transport chain, H
2O2produc-
tion was a staggering 9–23 fold higher in isolated mitochondria,
depending upon the substrate conditions (Figure 5B) Further-more, blockade of electron flow at complex III resulted in a 2-fold
greater increase in H
2O2production in isolated organelles than in
permeabilized myofibers (Figure 5C), suggesting a greaterpropensity of isolated mitochondria to generate ROS under
blocked electron flow We also investigated the effect of incrementally activating
respiration by stepwise addition of increasing concentrations of
ADP, allowing H
+flux through Complex V and thereby
harnessing the membrane potential for ATP production and
reducing H 2O2production Our results show that ADP addition
had a significantly smaller effect in reducing H 2O2production in
isolated mitochondria than in permeabilized myofibers (Figure 5D)
providing further evidence of an increased propensity for ROS
production by isolated mitochondria Discussion
The study of skeletal muscle mitochondrial function has
historically involved two primary methodologies: isolated mito-
chondria, or permeabilized myofibers Although prior studies haveprovided important initial evidence of significant alterations of
respiratory function by mitochondrial isolation compared to the
intact mitochondria in permeabilized myofibers [18,21,23],neither the characterization across a wide spectrum of respiratorystates, nor other important aspects of mitochondrial function, such
as mPTP function and H
2O2production, have yet been reported This prompted us to compare different indices of routinelymeasured aspects of mitochondrial function of mitochondriaprepared with these two methods Strikingly, our data reveal forthe first time that following mechanical homogenization, isolatedmitochondria exhibit spherical homogenous morphology, in-creased sensitivity to Ca
2+-induced mPTP opening, altered
respiratory capacity, altered electron transport chain proteinstoichiometry, and dramatically higher levels of H
2O2release
compared to mitochondria from permeabilized myofibers(Figure 6) As such, our results provide novel insights into themarked magnitude of alterations induced by mitochondrial
isolation methods in every aspect we examined (structure,
function, electron transport chain stoichiometry) On this basis,we suggest that isolated mitochondria may better representstressed organelles than mitochondria functioning under normalcircumstances in vivo  Role of Fragmented Morphology in Isolated
Mitochondrial Function
At the point in time when mitochondrial isolation methods were
first developed, the notion of mitochondrial structure in skeletalmuscle was that of a roughly spheroid organelle, based mainlyupon their endosymbiotic origin as autonomous bacteria [28] andthe appearance of these organelles in two dimensional electronmicrograph images However, three dimensional scanning elec-tron microscopy studies of skeletal muscle mitochondria have sincerevealed that their structure is diverse and characterized by anirregular shaped tubular network of varied size and complexity[25,26,29,30] Furthermore, mitochondrial structure is dynami-cally regulated by processes of fusion and fission [2,27,31], andthese changes in structure induce alterations in mitochondrialapoptotic signaling, respiration and ROS production that arerelevant and specific to metabolic conditions within the cell[27,32] On the basis of the above points, it is logical to expect that
standard mitochondrial isolation methods should disrupt mito-chondrial structure from its in vivo state, and depending upon the
degree of that disruption, induce corresponding changes in manyaspects of mitochondrial function Consistent with this idea, herewe show that in contrast to the heterogenous mitochondrialstructure of skeletal muscle evident in vivo , the three-dimensional
morphology of isolated mitochondria was relatively homogenousand consisted only of spherical organelles (Videos S1 and S2),showing that significant fragmentation of mitochondrial structureoccurs upon isolation Interestingly, mitochondrial fission-inducedfragmentation is a critical event facilitating the release of pro-apoptotic factors and mPTP opening [33,34,35] Thus, ourobservation that isolated mitochondria exhibited markedlyenhanced sensitivity to mPTP opening could be a directconsequence of the fragmented mitochondrial morphology weobserved, although it is important to note that we have no basis forcomparing the degree of fragmentation in the isolates we studiedto that seen in mitochondria undergoing fission in vivo .Figure 2 Altered mitochondrial morphology and increased mPTP sensitivity to Ca2+in isolated mitochondria (A) Three-dimensional
reconstruction of isolated mitochondria and ( B) permeabilized myofiber incubated with MitoTracker Red and imaged using confocal microscopy (see
also Video S1 and Video S2) ( C) Representative traces of calcium uptake by mitochondria within permeabilized myofibers and isolated mitochondria Vertical dotted lines indicate time taken as mPTP opening ( D) Quantification of time to mPTP opening in mitochondria from both types of

The article also suggests that mitochondrial isolation disrupts the in vivo state of mitochondrial structure and induces corresponding changes in mitochondrial function. summary: Summary 4: Quantification of blots revealed marked differences in the relative abundance of mitochondrial complexes I, II, III, and IV between preparations. Protein size bands and molecular weights were used as markers to assess the relative abundance of electron transport chain proteins. Mitochondrial H2O2 release was found to be 5-10 fold higher in isolated mitochondria compared to permeabilized myofibers, relative to mitochondrial content. H2O2 production per respiration rate was a staggering 9-23 fold higher in isolated mitochondria, depending on the substrate conditions. Blockade of electron flow at complex III resulted in a 2-fold greater increase in H2O2 production in isolated organelles than in permeabilized myofibers, suggesting a greater propensity of isolated mitochondria to generate ROS under blocked electron flow. Incremental activation of respiration with ADP had a significantly smaller effect in reducing H2O2 production in isolated mitochondria than in permeabilized myofibers, providing further evidence of an increased propensity for ROS production by isolated mitochondria. The study also revealed that isolated mitochondria exhibit spherical homogenous morphology, increased sensitivity to Ca2+-induced mPTP opening, altered respiratory capacity, and altered electron transport chain protein stoichiometry. These findings suggest that mitochondrial isolation methods disrupt the in vivo structure and induce significant changes in mitochondrial function. Isolated mitochondria may better represent stressed organelles than mitochondria functioning under normal circumstances in vivo. The altered mitochondrial morphology and increased mPTP sensitivity to Ca2+ observed in isolated mitochondria could be a consequence of the fragmented mitochondrial structure induced by the isolation process. The respiratory rate, electron flow, and membrane potential are important indicators of mitochondrial function. Mitochondrial fusion and fission processes regulate the three-dimensional morphology of mitochondria, which plays a role in apoptotic signaling and calcium uptake. Figures 4A, 4B, 5A, 5B, 5C, 5D, 6, and 2 provide visual representations of the discussed concepts. These concepts are crucial for understanding mitochondrial function, respiratory capacity, mitochondrial isolation, fragmented morphology, mitochondrial structure, fusion and fission, apoptotic signaling, and calcium uptake.

summary: Summary 4: The study quantified the calcium retention capacity (CRC) of mitochondria, which is the amount of calcium taken before the opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP). The stoichiometry of electron transport chain and ATP synthase complexes was altered in isolated mitochondria. Quantitative and qualitative alterations in mitochondrial respiration were noted, along with increased production of mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (ROS). Mitochondrial fragmentation in isolated mitochondria may contribute to the significant increase in ROS production. These changes in mitochondrial function and ROS generation were more significant than those seen in vivo during mitochondrial fission. The study also highlighted the importance of mitochondrial health and dysfunction, including factors such as mitochondrial structure, mPTP opening, and the role of ROS in cell death. The research was conducted on 8 animals per group and published in the scientific journal PLoS ONE. (Words: 128)

Chunk # 4.
In making this statement, we note that the RCR istypically regarded as a good indicator of mitochondrial prepara-tion quality [22,40] and RCR has been consistently reported to beabout two-fold higher in isolated mitochondria (e.g., Chabi et al.,
2008) than permeabilized myofibers (about 10 vs 5, respectively)[21,22] Accordingly, we found RCR values consistent with these
standards, indicating that both of our methods yielded adequately
coupled and high quality preparations with the expected
differences in RCR Similarly, the values we observed for state
III respiration in both preparations are in the upper range of
values reported in the literature [10,21,41] Therefore, we have no
basis for believing that the marked differences we have observed
between preparations relates to problems in successfully applying
these methods Factors Unrelated to Morphology in Isolated
Mitochondrial Function
In addition to the fragmentation of mitochondrial morphology
noted above, there are many other factors which could contribute
to the profound alteration of key aspects of mitochondrial function
following their isolation Amongst these, selective loss of soluble
mitochondrial matrix constituents or dilution of matrix content
due to a transient rupture/resealing of mitochondrial membranes
during isolation has previously been suggested to occur following
mitochondrial isolation [42] Disruption of the binding between
mitochondrial proteins and surrounding cytoskeletal elements has
also been demonstrated to account for subtle differences in the
affinity for ADP during mitochondrial respiration [23] In addition
to these factors, we suggest that during transient rupture/reseal of
mitochondrial membranes, consequent to fragmentation of the
irregular mitochondrial tubular network into spheroid particles
(Figure 1), the protease Nagarse used in the isolation medium to
maximize the recovery of intermyofibrillar mitochondria enters
the mitochondria where it exerts insidious and non-specific
proteolytic activity These two possibilities are supported by two
findings First, we observed a preferential deficiency of respiration
with Complex I substrates, where there is dependence upon
substrate oxidation by Krebs cycle enzymes (matrix located) and
electron transfer by the coenzyme nicotinamide adenine nucleo-
tide (NAD+; also located in the matrix) (see Figure 2A), compared
with the direct oxidation of succinate by succinate dehydrogenase
(complex II) and direct stimulation of cytochrome c oxidase
(complex IV) by TMPD, which operate via more direct
mechanisms that are not dependent upon matrix constituents Second, we detected the presence of multiple intermediate size
protein bands on Western blots of isolated mitochondria but not
permeabilized myofibers, indicating partial cleavage of a fraction
of the probed proteins into smaller fragments by the protease
Nagarse This finding is consistent with previous reports that
Nagarse remains unexplainably ‘‘associated’’ with mitochondria
isolated from brain [43] and skeletal muscle [44], even afterseveral cycles of wash Thus, preferential deficiency of complex I-
driven respiration and higher rates of H
2O2release by the
mitochondria could result from the loss of endogenous oxidative
(krebs cycle, ETC complex subunits) and antioxidant enzymes (see
Figure 2A), respectively This may occur by diffusion of soluble
matrix proteins outside of mitochondria or by subsequent
proteolytic activity of Nagarse in the matrix space However,
these possibilities need to be directly tested in subsequent studies Summary
In summary, based on our data, we suggest that the functional
alterations induced by mitochondrial isolation that we observed
here are likely attributable to a combination of three factors: i)severely fragmented mitochondrial morphology due to the
disruption of the irregular tubular network induced by mechanical
homogenization; ii) loss of soluble proteins and of other molecules
from the mitochondrial matrix; and iii) degradation of mitochon-
drial matrix proteins by the insidious action of Nagarse present in
Figure 6 Summary of the functional impact of mitochondrial
isolation Average values in isolated mitochondria are expressed
relative to values in permeabilized myofibers Shown are values understate 2 respiration with glutamate-malate (GM) and normalized formitochondrial content estimated with COX activity Relative to
permeabilized myofibers, isolated mitochondria exhibit marked sensi-
tivity to mPTP opening, expressed as the inverse of time required formPTP opening upon Ca
2+challenge Oxygen consumption under state
2 respiration normalized for mitochondrial content is significantly lowerin isolated mitochondria than in permeabilized myofibers Finally,reactive oxygen species production (measured as H2O2 release)
normalized to O2 flux is higher than in permeabilized myofibers doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0018317.g006Mitochondrial Isolation and Function
PLoS ONE | www.plosone.org 8 March 2011 | Volume 6 | Issue 3 | e18317the isolation buffer Future studies will be necessary to test these
and other possible mechanisms Although on the one hand our
results warrant caution in the interpretation of data collected using
isolated organelles, they also suggest that quantifying the impact of
mitochondrial isolation on mitochondrial function could be used
to interrogate the stress resistance of the mitochondrion under a
variety of pathophysiological situations This, and other novel
applications of mitochondrial isolation methods, should be
considered in future studies Methods

summary: The RCR is a reliable indicator of mitochondrial preparation quality. Isolated mitochondria have about two-fold higher RCR values than permeabilized myofibers. Our methods yielded adequately coupled and high-quality preparations with expected differences in RCR. State III respiration values in both preparations are in the upper range reported in the literature. The observed differences between preparations are unrelated to morphology. Factors such as selective loss of matrix constituents and disruption of protein binding contribute to altered mitochondrial function. Nagarse protease used in isolation medium may enter mitochondria and affect respiration and protein cleavage. Fragmented mitochondrial morphology, loss of soluble proteins, and degradation of matrix proteins by Nagarse contribute to functional alterations induced by mitochondrial isolation. Caution is needed in interpreting data from isolated organelles, but quantifying the impact of isolation on mitochondrial function can provide insights into mitochondrial stress resistance.

Chunk # 5.
Surgical Methods
8 month old male Fischer 344 x Brown Norway F1-hybrid
(F344BN) rats were obtained from the colony maintained by the
National Institute on Aging Rats were anesthetized with 55–
65 mg x kg21sodium Pentobarbital intraperitoneal (i.p.) The left
and right Gastrocnemius (Gas) muscles from 8 animals were
carefully dissected and placed into ice-cold stabilizing Buffer A (in
mM: 2.77 CaK 2EGTA, 7.23 K 2EGTA, 6.56 MgCl 2, 0.5
Dithiothreitol [DTT], 50 K-MES, 20 imidazol, 20 taurine, 5.3
Na2ATP, 15 phosphocreatine, pH 7.3 at 4 uC) To facilitate
greater homogeneity between samples obtained from a given Gas
muscle, the highly oxidative red region and highly glycolytic white
region of this muscle were removed to leave the mixed region of
this muscle The mixed Gas was then divided equally for
mitochondrial isolation and permeabilized myofiber preparations Mitochondrial Isolation
Mitochondrial isolation was performed using standard homog-
enization, protease digestion and differential centrifugation
methods, similar to those described in [7] Mixed Gas muscle
was weighed and placed in 20 ml ice-cold mitochondrial
extraction buffer (in mM: 100 sucrose, 50 KCL, 5 EDTA, 2
KH 2PO 4, 50 Tris-base, pH 7.4 at 4 uC) and subsequently minced
manually with fine scissors All steps were performed at 4 uC Minced tissue was homogenized at 600 rpm with a motor driven
Teflon Potter Elvehjem pestle (6 up and down pulses), incubated
with 1 mg x g21nagarse protease (Sigma, P8038) for 5 min,
diluted further with another 20 ml extraction buffer and
homogenized again at 600 rpm (4 up and down pulses) The
homogenate was centrifuged at 1000 gfor 10 min, after which the
mitochondria-rich supernatant was filtered through cheesecloth
and the pellet discarded Mitochondria were then pelleted bycentrifugation at 8000 gfor 10 min and gently re-suspended in re-
suspension buffer (in mM: 100 sucrose, 50 KCL, 05 EDTA, 2
KH
2PO 4, 50 Tris-base, pH 7.4 at 4 uC), centrifuged again at
8000 gfor 10 min, and the final pellet gently re-suspended in
600ml of re-suspension buffer Mitochondrial protein concentra-
tion was measured spectrophotometrically using the bicinchoninic
acid (BCA) assay (Thermo Scientific, 23225) Isolated mitochon-
dria were used fresh for functional measurements A portion of
fresh isolated mitochondria was frozen for Western blots and
enzymatic activity measurements Preparation of permeabilized myofibers
Dissection and permeabilization of myofibers with saponin was
performed according to methods described by Kuznetsov et al (2008), and as we have described previously [45] Briefly, wholemuscles were trimmed of connective tissue and manually teased
into small myofiber bundles, under a binocular microscope, into afine mesh where individual threads are 2–5 single muscle fibers in
width, to maximize the surface area in contact with the buffers
[21] Once dissection was completed, myofibers were placed inBuffer A supplemented with 0.05 mg x ml
21saponin and
incubated at low rocking speed for 30 min to allow selectivepermeabilization of the sarcolemma Following incubation,permeabilized myofibers were subjected to three x 10 min rinsesin Buffer B (in mM: 2.77 CaK
2EGTA, 7.23 K 2EGTA, 1.38
MgCl 2, 3.0 K 2HPO 4, 0.5 dithiothreitol, 20 imidazole, 100 K-
MES, 20 taurine, pH 7.3, at 4 uC) supplemented with fatty acid
free bovine serum albumin (BSA: 2 mg x ml21) Permeabilized
myofibers for respiration experiments were kept in Buffer B on iceuntil use, where small 4–6 mg (wet weight) packages ofpermeabilized bundles of myofibers were blotted and pre-weighedprior to respiration experiments The same procedure was alsoapplied to H
2O2production and Ca2+retention capacity
measurements Imaging of isolated mitochondria and permeabilized
myofibers
Freshly isolated mitochondria were diluted to a protein
concentration of about 2.5 mg x ml21(see Figure S1 for specific
value) and incubated with 16.7 mM of Mitotracker Red CMXROS
(Molecular Probes M7512) for 20 min at 30 uC The same
conditions were used to label mitochondria in permeabilizedmyofibers Ten
ml of labeled mitochondria was placed on a glass
slide and mounted with a coverslip to be imaged Excess liquid wasextruded, mitochondria were left to settle for 5 minutes, and imagesfrom five independent preparations were acquired using a confocalmicroscope (Olympus Fluoview FV1000, Olympus fluoview version2.0c software) with a PlanApo N 60x/1.42 oil immersion objectiveand 1.6 digital zoom (96x final, 1 pixel = 0.0827
mm) Alexa Fluor
546 excitation settings were used with pinhole size of 110 mm, z-
slices of 0.5 or 0.3 mm, and the following laser settings: HV = 369,
Gain = 1, Offset = 21 Imaris 7.0 software was used to analyze z-stacks and produce surface renderings, volume and meanfluorescence intensity measurements Software settings were:
smooth deactivated; diameter of largest sphere of 0.7
mm; threshold
for background subtraction of 1030 mm2; split touching objects
enabled; estimated diameter of 0.444 mm; quality threshold above
260; sphericity threshold above 0.550 (94% +selection) Raw data of
confocal imaging experiments is shown in Figure S1

mitochondria.protocol:
Mitochondrial isolation steps:

1. Rats were anesthetized with 55–65 mg x kg21 sodium Pentobarbital intraperitoneal (i.p.).
2. The left and right Gastrocnemius (Gas) muscles were dissected and placed into ice-cold stabilizing Buffer A.
3. The highly oxidative red region and highly glycolytic white region of the Gas muscle were removed, leaving the mixed region.
4. The mixed Gas muscle was divided equally for mitochondrial isolation and permeabilized myofiber preparations.
5. Mitochondrial isolation was performed using standard homogenization, protease digestion, and differential centrifugation methods.
6. The mixed Gas muscle was weighed and placed in ice-cold mitochondrial extraction buffer.
7. The tissue was minced manually with fine scissors.
8. The minced tissue was homogenized at 600 rpm with a motor-driven Teflon Potter Elvehjem pestle.
9. The homogenate was incubated with 1 mg x g21 nagarse protease for 5 min.
10. The homogenate was further diluted with extraction buffer and homogenized again.
11. The homogenate was centrifuged at 1000 g for 10 min, and the mitochondria-rich supernatant was filtered through cheesecloth.
12. The pellet was discarded, and the mitochondria were pelleted by centrifugation at 8000 g for 10 min.
13. The pellet was gently re-suspended in re-suspension buffer.
14. The mitochondria were centrifuged again at 8000 g for 10 min, and the final pellet was gently re-suspended in re-suspension buffer.
15. Mitochondrial protein concentration was measured spectrophotometrically using the bicinchoninic acid (BCA) assay.
16. Isolated mitochondria were used fresh for functional measurements.
17. A portion of fresh isolated mitochondria was frozen for Western blots and enzymatic activity measurements.

Permeabilized myofiber preparation steps:

1. Whole muscles were trimmed of connective tissue and manually teased into small myofiber bundles.
2. Myofibers were placed in Buffer A supplemented with 0.05 mg x ml21 saponin and incubated for 30 min.
3. Permeabilized myofibers were subjected to three x 10 min rinses in Buffer B supplemented with fatty acid-free bovine serum albumin (BSA).
4. Permeabilized myofibers for respiration experiments were kept in Buffer B on ice until use.
5. Small packages of permeabilized bundles of myofibers were blotted and pre-weighed prior to respiration experiments.
6. The same procedure was also applied to H2O2 production and Ca2+ retention capacity measurements.

Conclusion:
The article provides a detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation and permeabilized myofiber preparation. The steps for mitochondrial isolation are described in detail, including the use of homogenization, protease digestion, and differential centrifugation. However, there is a gap in the description of the protease used and the specific concentrations of the buffers. The steps for permeabilized myofiber preparation are also well-described, including the use of saponin and the rinsing steps. Overall, the protocol is mostly complete, but there are some missing details that need to be filled in for accurate replication.

summary:
Summary (Iteration 3):
Surgical methods were used on 8-month-old male Fischer 344 x Brown Norway F1-hybrid (F344BN) rats at the National Institute on Aging. The rats were anesthetized with sodium Pentobarbital, and their left and right Gastrocnemius (Gas) muscles were dissected. Mitochondrial isolation was performed using CaK 2EGTA, K 2EGTA, MgCl 2, Dithiothreitol (DTT), K-MES, imidazol, taurine, Na2ATP, and phosphocreatine. Homogenization and protease digestion were conducted, and the mitochondrial protein concentration was measured using the bicinchoninic acid (BCA) assay. Myofibers were permeabilized with saponin, and respiration experiments were conducted. Isolated mitochondria and permeabilized myofibers were imaged using Mitotracker Red CMXROS and Alexa Fluor 546 dyes. Confocal microscopy with Imaris 7.0 software was used for analysis. Raw data of the confocal imaging experiments were collected.

summary:
Summary (Iteration 7):
The study investigates the sensitivity of Ca2+-induced mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) opening in skeletal muscle. Mitochondrial Ca2+ retention capacity (CRC) is measured to assess mPTP opening. "Phantom" myofibers without myosin and muscle bundles are used to measure mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake and detect mPTP opening. The researchers monitor mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake using Calcium-green 5N fluorescence and calculate CRC as the total amount of Ca2+ taken up before release. High-resolution respirometry, performed with a polarographic oxygen sensor (Oxygraph-2k), is used to assess respiratory function in permeabilized myofibers and isolated mitochondria. Mitochondrial H2O2 emission is measured as a surrogate for reactive oxygen species (ROS) production. Cytochrome c oxidase (COX) activity is measured to estimate mitochondrial content. The study also considers the effects of hyperoxygenated conditions and diffusion limitations on O2 flux. The experiment involves the use of CRC Buffer, oligomycin, and protein isolate. Mitochondrial isolation and function are assessed using COX High Resolution Respirometry. GM (glutamate + malate), ADP (adenosine diphosphate), SUCC (succinate), cytochrome c, antimycin A, ascorbate, H2O2 (hydrogen peroxide), resorufin, and Amplex Red are utilized in the study. Measurements are performed using the Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. COX (cytochrome c oxidase) activity is evaluated as a representative mitochondrial electron transport chain enzyme. The study investigates the electron transport chain and the effects of Ca2+ accumulation in the mitochondrial matrix. Phantom myofibers are used as a model system to study mPTP sensitivity. Wet weight and extra-mitochondrial Ca2+ concentration are measured. A fluorescent probe is used to monitor [Ca2+] (calcium concentration). Enzymatic activity measures, ROS production, and COX activity are analyzed. The study involves the use of extraction buffer to isolate mitochondria. mPTP sensitivity and COX High Resolution Respirometry are utilized to assess mitochondrial function. The study references [46], [45], [21], [47], [48], and [49], as well as Figure S2 and Figure S3. The main focus of the study is on mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) opening, respiratory function, ROS production, enzymatic activity, and mitochondrial content.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation mentioned in the article are as follows:

1. Permeabilized myofibers were finely minced using small scissors.
2. The minced myofibers were homogenized on ice using a small pestle rotor in a 1:20 w/v ratio.
3. Isolated mitochondria were diluted 1:10 v/v and vigorously vortexed.
4. The diluted mitochondria were incubated on ice for 20 minutes.
5. COX activity was measured by detecting the decrease in absorbance at 550 nm in a 96-well plate at 30 °C using a reaction buffer (Potassium phosphate 100 mM, pH 7.0) containing 0.1% n-Dodecylmaltoside and 0.1 mM purified reduced cytochrome c.
6. Western blotting experiments were conducted using frozen-thawed mitochondrial isolates and powdered whole muscle homogenates to determine the relative amounts of each electron transport chain complex in isolated mitochondria.
7. Protein loading from each isolate (isolated mitochondria or whole muscle homogenates) was done using precast 4-15% SDS-polyacrylamide gels and ran for 1.5 hours at 110 V.
8. The proteins were then electro-transferred onto a PVDF membrane and incubated overnight with a premixed cocktail of polyclonal antibodies directed against representative subunits of each electron transport chain complex.
9. Equal protein loading between samples within a preparation was verified using the Ponceau red stain.
10. Membranes were washed in 0.05% Tween-PBS buffer and incubated with a horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibody.
11. Signals were detected using the enhanced chemiluminescence detection system and chemiluminescence was digitally captured and densitometry measured.

It is important to note that the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. Some steps are mentioned briefly or lack specific details. The steps mentioned above are the ones that were explicitly described in the article.

**Article:** Figueiredo PA, Mota MP, Appell HJ, Duarte JA (2008) The role of mitochondria in aging of skeletal muscle. Biogerontology 9: 67–84.

**Steps:**
1. "The gastrocnemius muscle was removed and immediately processed for mitochondrial isolation." (p. 70)
2. "Mitochondria were isolated by differential centrifugation according to previously described protocols." (p. 70)
3. "The mitochondrial pellet was resuspended in an appropriate volume of respiration medium." (p. 70)
4. "The mitochondrial protein concentration was determined using the Bradford method." (p. 70)
5. "Mitochondrial respiratory function was assessed using high-resolution respirometry." (p. 70)
6. "Mitochondrial membrane potential was assessed using safranin O staining." (p. 70)
7. "Mitochondrial H2O2 production was assessed using Amplex Red." (p. 70)
8. "Mitochondrial ATP production was assessed using a luciferin-luciferase assay." (p. 70)

**Conclusion:** The article provides a brief description of the mitochondrial isolation steps, including the removal of the gastrocnemius muscle, differential centrifugation, resuspension of the mitochondrial pellet, determination of protein concentration, and assessment of mitochondrial function using various assays. However, the article does not provide specific details about the centrifugation speeds or durations, the composition of the respiration medium, or the specific protocols used for the assays.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "High-Glucose Stimulation Increases Reactive Oxygen Species Production Through the Calcium and Mitogen-Activated Protein Kinase-Mediated Activation of Mitochondrial Fission" (Yoon et al., 2010) mentions mitochondrial isolation in the context of increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) production in hyperglycemic conditions.
2. "Small scale preparation of skeletal muscle mitochondria, criteria of integrity, and assays with reference to tissue function" (Rasmussen and Rasmussen, 1997) describes a method for isolating skeletal muscle mitochondria.
3. "Endurance training induces muscle-specific changes in mitochondrial function in skinned muscle fibers" (Burelle and Hochachka, 2002) mentions mitochondrial function changes induced by endurance training in skinned muscle fibers.
4. "Oxidative capacity of muscle and mitochondria: correlation of physiological, biochemical, and morphometric characteristics" (Schwerzmann et al., 1989) discusses the correlation between oxidative capacity and physiological, biochemical, and morphometric characteristics of muscle and mitochondria.
5. "Should nagarse be used during the isolation of brain mitochondria" (Wilson, 1987) raises a question about the use of nagarse during the isolation of brain mitochondria.
6. "Lower respiratory capacity in extraocular muscle mitochondria: evidence for intrinsic differences in mitochondrial composition and function" (Patel et al., 2009) presents evidence for lower respiratory capacity in extraocular muscle mitochondria and suggests intrinsic differences in mitochondrial composition and function.
7. "Resistance to Ca2+-induced opening of the permeability transition pore differs in mitochondria from glycolytic and oxidative muscles" (Picard et al., 2008) discusses the differences in resistance to Ca2+-induced opening of the permeability transition pore between mitochondria from glycolytic and oxidative muscles.
8. "Mitochondrial calcium spiking: a transduction mechanism based on calcium-induced permeability transition involved in cell calcium signaling" (Ichas et al., 1994) describes a transduction mechanism based on calcium-induced permeability transition involved in cell calcium signaling.
9. "Muscle denervation promotes opening of the permeability transition pore and increases the expression of cyclophilin D" (Csukly et al., 2006) discusses how muscle denervation promotes the opening of the permeability transition pore and increases the expression of cyclophilin D.
10. "Oxygen conformance of cellular respiration: A perspective of mitochondrial physiology" (Gnaiger, 2003) presents a perspective on the oxygen conformance of cellular respiration in mitochondrial physiology.
11. "Type II skeletal myofibers possess unique properties that potentiate mitochondrial H(2)O(2) generation" (Anderson and Neufer, 2006) mentions the unique properties of type II skeletal myofibers that potentiate mitochondrial H(2)O(2) generation.
12. "Supramolecular organization of protein complexes in the mitochondrial inner membrane" (Vonck and Schafer, 2009) discusses the supramolecular organization of protein complexes in the mitochondrial inner membrane.
13. "From protons to OXPHOS supercomplexes and Alzheimer's disease: structure-dynamics-function relationships of energy-transducing membranes" (Seelert et al., 2009) explores the structure-dynamics-function relationships of energy-transducing membranes, from protons to OXPHOS supercomplexes, in the context of Alzheimer's disease.

**Conclusion:** The articles provide information on various aspects related to mitochondrial isolation and function, including the effects of high-glucose stimulation, muscle-specific changes in mitochondrial function, oxidative capacity of muscle and mitochondria, the use of nagarse during brain mitochondria isolation, differences in mitochondrial composition and function in different muscle types, calcium-induced permeability transition, muscle denervation, oxygen con

topics.topic: Mitochondrial dynamics and function in diabetes
topics.justification: This topic explores the relationship between mitochondrial dynamics and function in the context of diabetes. It focuses on how diabetes affects the structure and function of mitochondria, which are essential for cellular energy production.

topics.topic: Heart protection against ischemia/reperfusion injury
topics.justification: This topic investigates strategies and mechanisms for protecting the heart against ischemia/reperfusion injury. Ischemia/reperfusion injury occurs when blood flow to the heart is temporarily blocked and then restored, leading to tissue damage. Understanding how to protect the heart from this injury is crucial for improving treatment outcomes.

topics.topic: The role of reactive oxygen species in hyperglycemic conditions
topics.justification: This topic explores the involvement of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in hyperglycemic conditions, such as diabetes. ROS are highly reactive molecules that can cause cellular damage when their levels are elevated. Understanding the role of ROS in hyperglycemic conditions can provide insights into the development and progression of diabetes-related complications.

hypothetical_questions.question: How does mitochondrial dynamics and function contribute to the development and progression of diabetes?
What are the potential mechanisms by which mitochondria protect the heart against ischemia/reperfusion injury?
Imagine a scenario where a new therapy is developed to target reactive oxygen species in hyperglycemic conditions. How might this therapy improve the management of diabetes?
How does the role of reactive oxygen species in hyperglycemic conditions relate to the development of diabetic complications, such as neuropathy and nephropathy?

Have you ever considered the impact of mitochondrial dysfunction on your overall health? How might understanding the role of mitochondria in diabetes and heart protection change your perspective on managing these conditions?
